medications.indication_rationale: Reduces the cutaneous lupus inflammation and burning; a low-potency steroid is used on the face.
Strict daily photoprotection is central to preventing cutaneous lupus flares.
No cellulitis; antibiotics are not indicated.
A rheumatology decision for recurrent cutaneous lupus, not an acute telehealth initiation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a cool compress then the steroid cream, avoid the sun tomorrow, and use SPF 50+ daily (even indoors near windows).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cools the skin and prevents further UV-triggered flares.

patient_education_points.point: This is a sun-triggered lupus skin rash (the 'butterfly' rash) — a flare of the skin, not a sign your organs are being attacked. It skips the creases beside your nose and you have no fever, chest or kidney symptoms.
Use a cool compress and a mild steroid cream, avoid the sun tomorrow, and wear SPF 50+ every day. See your GP/rheumatologist within a week.

Go to ED if you get sharp chest pain on breathing or breathlessness, your urine turns cola/red-coloured with ankle swelling, the redness spreads into your eyes with pain/vision loss, or you develop a high fever.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Confirm cutaneous lupus, photoprotection, consider hydroxychloroquine; baseline organ screen.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Serositis, nephritis, facial cellulitis, or ocular involvement.

must_include_items: Exclude facial cellulitis (nasolabial sparing/bilateral/afebrile) and organ crisis (serositis/nephritis) before reassuring
Cool compress + mild topical corticosteroid + strict photoprotection
errors_of_commission: Antibiotics for a nasolabial-sparing bilateral malar rash
ED over-triage of an afebrile cutaneous flare
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T1/T2 case: passing requires a cellulitis/organ-crisis screen + cool compress/topical steroid/photoprotection + safety-net. Antibiotics/ED over-triage is a failure.
